text: PostgreSQL 16 says client authentication is controlled by the pg_hba.conf file in the cluster data directory unless hba_file points elsewhere. The file is read as one record per line. For a connection attempt, PostgreSQL uses the first record whose connection type, client address, database, and user all match. There is no fallback to later records if authentication with that first matching record fails. If no record matches at all, access is denied.

In PostgreSQL 16, a pg_hba.conf entry can be an authentication rule or an include directive. The supported include directives are include, include_if_exists, and include_dir. Included content is inserted where the directive appears. For include_dir, PostgreSQL reads files that do not start with a dot and do end with .conf, and it processes them in file-name order using C locale ordering.

PostgreSQL 16 supports local, host, hostssl, hostnossl, hostgssenc, and hostnogssenc record types. local is for Unix-domain sockets. host matches TCP/IP whether or not SSL or GSSAPI encryption is used. hostssl matches only SSL connections and requires both PostgreSQL to be built with SSL support and the ssl setting to be enabled. hostgssenc matches only GSSAPI-encrypted TCP/IP connections and requires GSSAPI support. Remote TCP/IP access also depends on listen_addresses, because the default server behavior only listens on the loopback address localhost.

In PostgreSQL 16, the database field can match all, sameuser, samerole, replication, specific database names, or regular expressions. The user field can match all, a specific user, a role name prefixed with plus, or a regular expression. For both database and user fields, a value starting with slash is treated as a regular expression, multiple names or regexes can be separated by commas, and a file prefixed with at-sign can supply names or regexes from another file.

The address field in PostgreSQL 16 can use CIDR-style IP ranges, explicit IP address plus mask columns, host names, or the keywords all, samehost, and samenet. samehost matches the server's own IP addresses, and samenet matches addresses in directly connected subnets. When a host name is used, PostgreSQL first checks reverse resolution of the client IP to a host name, then does a forward lookup of that host name to confirm the client address. A host name beginning with a dot matches a suffix such as foo.example.com.

The auth-method field selects how a matching connection is authenticated. PostgreSQL 16 lists methods including trust, reject, scram-sha-256, md5, password, gss, sspi, ident, peer, ldap, radius, cert, pam, and bsd. trust allows the connection without checking credentials, while reject blocks it unconditionally. ident is only for TCP/IP and uses the client ident service; if ident is specified for a local connection, PostgreSQL uses peer instead. peer is available only for local connections. hostssl rules can also use the clientcert option to require a trusted client certificate.

The order of pg_hba.conf records matters because PostgreSQL checks them sequentially and stops at the first matching entry. On most systems, changes to pg_hba.conf are applied after startup only when the server receives SIGHUP, for example through pg_ctl reload or pg_reload_conf(). On Microsoft Windows, new connections see file changes immediately. PostgreSQL 16 also notes that pg_hba_file_rules can help diagnose load problems, and that passing pg_hba.conf checks is not enough by itself because the user still needs CONNECT privilege on the target database.

In PostgreSQL 15, the database field can match all, sameuser, samerole, replication, specific database names, or a file prefixed with at-sign. The user field can match all users, a specific user, a role name prefixed with plus, or a file prefixed with at-sign. This older version description does not treat slash-prefixed database or user values as regular expressions.
